title: Merck — RSV — Thesis
html:
Investment Thesis: Enflonsia in Infant RSV Prevention

Governing Thought

Beyfortus's first-mover advantage in infant RSV is durable but not invincible. Enflonsia cannot win on clinical data (60% vs. 75% MA-LRTI reduction), but it can win on operational simplicity at the practice level -- and in a seasonal product category where stocking decisions are binary ("which one do I order this year?"), convenience advantages compound into market share over 2-3 seasons. The thesis: Enflonsia reaches 25-35% share by the 2027-28 season through pre-season operational messaging, not by out-detailing Beyfortus on efficacy.

The Case FOR Enflonsia Taking Share

1. The Practice Decision Is Binary, Not Gradual

Most pediatric practices stock only one RSV mAb (barrier data: "practice policy -- only stock one RSV product"). This means Enflonsia doesn't need to split prescriptions; it needs to flip entire practices. Each flip is a 100% share swing at that site. The relevant metric is practice-level formulary wins, not prescription-level share.

2. Operational Differentiation Is Underpriced

In a category where both products protect for 5 months at $556/dose with ACIP recommendation and VFC eligibility, the clinical story is "good enough on both sides." What tips the stocking decision:

  • One dose, one SKU -- No weight calculation, no risk of administering wrong dose. In a busy pediatric practice doing well-child visits, this matters more than a 15-point efficacy delta on a clinical trial.
  • 30-month shelf life + 48-hour room temp -- Reduces waste, simplifies inventory. Beyfortus shelf life and cold-chain requirements are stricter.
  • Supply reliability -- Beyfortus's 2023 shortage is still in practice memory. "We have supply" is a message that carries weight.

3. The Pre-Season Window Is the Entire Battleground

RSV stocking decisions happen July-September. Whoever wins that window wins the season. Beyfortus's real-world evidence advantage matters less during the ordering window than "can I get it, is it easy to stock, and does my staff know how to use it."

4. Beyfortus Has Structural Vulnerabilities

  • Weight-based dosing creates waste -- 50 mg and 100 mg SKUs mean practices must stock both; miscalculation risk exists
  • 2023 shortage memory -- Even though resolved, trust was damaged
  • Real-world evidence is a double-edged sword -- As more RWE accumulates, any signal (however minor) gets amplified. FDA's Dec 2025 safety investigation into both products creates uncertainty.
  • Price parity eliminates economic moat -- At identical pricing, the cost argument disappears

5. ACIP Gave Enflonsia Full Legitimacy

No preferential recommendation means ACIP is telling pediatricians: "pick either one." This is the regulatory floor Enflonsia needed. AAP alignment (which HCPs trust more than CDC) further normalizes the choice.

The Case AGAINST Enflonsia Taking Share

1. Inertia Is the Strongest Force in Medicine

"Committed to/currently using Beyfortus" is the #1 barrier, and it's structural, not addressable through messaging. Practices that survived the 2023 shortage and have 2 seasons of Beyfortus experience have no rational reason to switch unless something goes wrong.

2. The Efficacy Gap Is Real and Visible

74.9% vs. 60.5% MA-LRTI reduction. Even though trials aren't head-to-head, HCPs see these numbers. In PET data, HCPs associate efficacy messages more with Beyfortus than Enflonsia. This perception gap widens as Beyfortus accumulates RWE.

3. Real-World Evidence Creates an Unbridgeable Lead

40+ studies and 6M+ infants vs. zero RWE for Enflonsia. This asymmetry takes 3-5 years to close. HCPs rate Beyfortus's real-world data messages (BEYF12, BEYF10) among the most effective -- and Enflonsia has nothing to counter with.

4. Beyfortus's Second-Season Indication Is a Moat

Beyfortus is already approved for children <24 months at increased risk through second RSV season. Enflonsia is filing based on SMART trial (Feb 2026), but until approved, practices covering high-risk infants must use Beyfortus for second-season protection. This creates a "why switch when I need Beyfortus anyway" dynamic.

5. Reach Gap Is Massive and Growing

Beyfortus reaches 80% of MDs via PP vs. 50% for Enflonsia. NPP gap is 95% vs. 41%. Enflonsia reps trail on every performance metric. You can't win a market you can't reach.

6. The CDC Schedule Changes Create Headwinds for Everyone

2/3 of HCPs expect fewer infants will receive RSV prevention (driven by parent refusal). A shrinking pie makes share capture harder -- Merck is fighting for a smaller market.

Net Assessment

Factor Advantage Magnitude
Clinical efficacy (MA-LRTI) Beyfortus Large (75% vs. 60%)
Real-world evidence Beyfortus Massive (40+ studies vs. 0)
First-mover / inertia Beyfortus Large
Second-season indication Beyfortus Moderate (Enflonsia filing pending)
HCP reach (PP + NPP) Beyfortus Large
Rep performance Beyfortus Moderate
Dosing simplicity Enflonsia Moderate-Large
Storage/handling Enflonsia Moderate
Supply reliability Enflonsia Moderate (diminishing as Beyfortus supply stabilizes)
Price Parity --
ACIP recommendation Parity --

Bottom line: Beyfortus holds the structural advantage. Enflonsia's best path is a 3-season penetration strategy targeting the operational convenience-sensitive segment (smaller practices, solo practitioners, practices that experienced Beyfortus supply issues). Realistic share target: 15-20% by end of 2025-26 season, 25-35% by 2027-28 if second-season indication is secured and RWE begins to accumulate.

What ZoomRx Should Be Tracking

Leading Indicators (predictive of share movement)

  1. Pre-season stocking decisions -- Practice-level formulary choices, July-September each year
  2. Enflonsia stocking likelihood trend -- Currently ~33% high LTIS; needs to reach 45%+ to signal momentum
  3. Barrier shift -- "Committed to Beyfortus" barrier declining (-26% Q4->Q1) is a positive signal
  4. Message effectiveness trends -- ENF2 dominance must hold; watch for efficacy message fatigue
  5. RWE introduction timing -- When does Merck first include real-world data in rep materials?

Lagging Indicators (confirming share movement)

  1. Beyfortus revenue trajectory -- Any deceleration signals Enflonsia impact
  2. VFC distribution split -- State-level VFC ordering data (public)
  3. Insurance formulary positioning -- Any payer preferences emerging?
  4. CDC/ACIP coverage data -- Next MMWR report on RSV immunization rates

Risk Monitors

  1. FDA safety investigation -- Dec 2025 investigation into both products; any adverse finding asymmetry matters
  2. Parent refusal trends -- CDC schedule changes + anti-vax sentiment could shrink total market
  3. Beyfortus 6-month duration claim -- EU label already says 6 months; US label update would widen gap
  4. Enflonsia second-season filing timeline -- Delays here extend Beyfortus's moat
